messages.content:
Task
Write a video script for a 2 minute (400-500 words) Rust news video.
Steps
1. Find a Rust related news story (that Bogdan has not covered yet!). The news does NOT
have to be recent. Here are some places you can look:
a. The Rust sub-reddit
b. The official Rust blog
c. Google news
d. HackerNews
2. Come up with an engaging title for the video (and note it down somewhere)
3. Use the video script template below to write a 2 minute video (400-500 words) script.
a. Look at the Example Scripts folder for reference. These scripts may contain
incomplete sentences, and grammar/spelling mistakes because they were written
and read by the same author. Your script should have complete sentences and
correct grammar/spelling because they will be read by someone else.
b. Focus on making the script engaging. The goal is to keep the viewers' attention.
c. Keep in mind that this script should be written for Bogdan to read. Please feel
free to look at the Rust news videos to get an idea of how Bogdan speaks.

4. Double check your grammar and spelling.
5. Email your script to <PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED_EMAIL_ADDRESS>.
a. The script should be attached as a PDF document.
b. The PDF file name should be the title of the video.
c. The subject of your email should be “Technical Writer Test Task Submission”.
d. NOTE: The script should be submitted within a week of you getting the test task
email.

Notes
● Feel free to use AI tools like ChatGPT to help you write. These tools don’t create great
sounding scripts, but they can give you a head start.
● If you have any additional questions please email <PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED_EMAIL_ADDRESS> with the
subject “Technical Writer Test Task Question”.
Please read the instructions above carefully before submitting your script. We will automatically
reject people who do not follow the instructions (ex: having the right email subject, submitting a
PDF document, etc.).
If we choose to move forward with your application you will receive an email within 2 weeks of
your submission. If we choose not to move forward with your application you will not receive any
email. Please do not contact us about the status of your application. If we have other open
positions in the future we will reach out to you!
Good luck and I hope we get a chance to work together! :) - Bogdan

Video Script Template
● Intro
○ Hook
■ The goal is to grab the viewer's attention in the first couple of seconds.
■ Using short words or phrases is preferable.
○ Retention hook
■ The job of the retention hook is to make the viewer want to watch the
entire video.
■ A retention hook can be a question that will be answered at the end of the
video, or a statement about something that will be revealed at the end.

● Body
○ History / Context / Background
■ This is the “meat” of the video. The part viewers watch in anticipation of
the retention pay-off.
■ For news videos you can include history, context, or background that
helps the viewer better understand the news story.

○ Transition
■ This should be a short transition that prepares viewers for the retention
pay-off.

● Outro
○ Retention pay-off
■ This is where you give the viewers what you promised in the retention
hook.
■ For news videos this can be the news story itself.
○ Commentary (hope, concern, etc.)
■ Give viewers commentary on the news. Is this news good for Rust?
Should viewers be concerned? Remember that this will be Bogdan
talking.
○ CTA
■ This is where Bogdan gives viewers a call to action (ex: download the
cheat sheet, leave a comment, like the video, etc) and closes out the
video. Refer to the Example Script folder for reference.

i want a script to talk about axum in a manner you mention in the beginning that is the end of rocket most rust framework

Title: "Is Axum the Future, Eclipse Rocket?"
PDF Filename: Is_Axum_the_Future_Eclipse_Rocket.pdf

Video Script:

[Intro]
Hook:
"Rustaceans, brace yourselves—we've got a framework on the horizon that could shake up the whole ecosystem!"

Retention hook:
"Keep watching to find out if Axum, the rising star in Rust's web development scene, is truly poised to outshine the beloved Rocket framework."

[Body]
History / Context / Background:
Rust enthusiasts, we've all appreciated the power and reliability Rocket brought to web development in Rust. Offering a web framework with a focus on ease-of-use, expressibility, and speed, Rocket has long been the go-to for Rustaceans looking to build web services. 

However, a new challenger approaches, promising async support and a tower-based foundation. Enter Axum—developed by the Tokio team, the same brilliant minds behind the asynchronous runtime that's become integral to Rust's async story. Unlike Rocket's built-in async handling, Axum squarely leverages Rust's async ecosystem, aiming for seamless integration and top-notch performance.

Transition:
So, is Axum just another framework, or does it have the rocket fuel to soar past its namesake?

[Outro]
Retention pay-off:
Axum, with its explicit focus on async-await paradigms, modularity, and type safety, is not just making waves—it's setting a new horizon for Rust web frameworks. While Rocket isn't out of the race, the entrance of Axum represents an exciting shift towards a more mature async ecosystem within Rust.

Commentary (hope, concern, etc.):
As a Rust advocate, I'm hopeful and excited. It's not just about one framework superseding another; it's about Rust's growth and evolution. Frameworks like Axum signal a community that's actively innovating and embracing Rust's strengths. It's a win for all Rustaceans, as we benefit from diverse, robust tools that push the boundaries of what's possible.

Of course, with every new technology, there's a period of uncertainty. How will Axum integrate with existing projects? Will it become the new standard, and what does that mean for the future of Rocket? Only time will tell, but one thing is certain—the Rust community thrives on progress and Axum is a product of that relentless push forward.

CTA:
So, what are your thoughts on Axum's rise? Is it truly the herald of a new era for Rust web development, or will Rocket hold its ground? Drop your thoughts in the comments below, and if you're excited to see where Rust's web frameworks are heading, leave a like, and don't forget to subscribe for more Rust news and insights. Until next time, keep on Rusting!

[END OF SCRIPT]

Please remember to email the script as a PDF document titled "Is_Axum_the_Future_Eclipse_Rocket.pdf" to <PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED_EMAIL_ADDRESS> with the subject line “Technical Writer Test Task Submission.” 

Best of luck!

Bogdan: Now, let's get down to business and address the comparison between Axum and Rocket. Recently, Rocket has seen a major update, and while it remains a popular choice for Rust developers, it still needs to catch up to the latest advancements introduced by Axum. The combination of Axum's sleek design and Tokio's runtime capabilities sets a new standard that Rocket will need to match.

Bogdan: Can Rocket reach the level of updates and innovation that Axum delivers? As both frameworks continue to evolve, it will be fascinating to see how Rocket responds to the challenges of staying on par with Axum's revolutionary approach to Rust web development.

Add this to the article and make correct the grammer

Title: "Axum vs. Rocket: The Showdown in Rust's Web Development Arena"
PDF Filename: Axum_vs_Rocket_Showdown_in_Rust_Web_Development.pdf

Video Script:

[Intro]
Hook:
"Fasten your seatbelts, Rustaceans—it's showdown time in the web framework corral!"

Retention Hook:
"Is Axum the forward-thinking hero Rust has been waiting for, or will Rocket's recent updates keep it flying high? Stay tuned to find out!"

[Body]
History / Context / Background:
Rust's web development landscape has been bustling with activity lately. Rocket, a long-standing heavyweight champion in the arena, is known for its usability and comprehensive feature set. However, the framework landscape is ever-shifting, and with change comes new challengers. 

Enter Axum, born from the creators of Tokio, and armed with cutting-edge async capabilities and a design that maximizes Rust's async programming features. It's clear Axum was built for Rust's future in mind.

Bogdan's Commentary on Axum vs. Rocket:
Now, let's delve into the nitty-gritty. Rocket recently blasted through with a major update, maintaining its popularity among the Rust community. However, it's facing the pressure to stay relevant with the async tide brought in by Axum.

The blend of Axum's modern approach and the backing of Tokio's robust async runtime sets a formidable benchmark—a bar Rocket has now found itself striving to reach.

Transition:
So, with the stage set for an epic fray, let's weigh in on the latest developments and see just how these titans are matching up.

[Outro]
Retention Pay-Off:
Can Rocket ascend to the level of innovation and refinement that Axum serves up? As both frameworks advance, we're all witnesses to this enthralling race. Rocket is certainly not bowing out; it's adapting and reinventing. Yet, Axum is steadily carving out a future-ready path, becoming the beacon for Rust's web development aspirations.

Bogdan's Reflective Commentary:
In the ever-evolving story of Rust, it's not just about one framework outpacing the other—it's a tale of progress, choice, and adaptability. Whether you're team Rocket or team Axum, what truly matters is the remarkable progress we're seeing unfold. 

As developers and enthusiasts, let’s embrace the changes and support the growth of the Rust ecosystem in all its forms. And to the frameworks themselves—may the best tool win, for the benefit of all Rustaceans!

CTA:
But now, I'm turning it over to you—where do you stand in the Axum vs. Rocket saga? Ignite the comments with your opinions and insights, and let's get a fiery discussion going! And if you're all in for more Rust battles and breakthroughs, hit that like button, and smash subscribe. Until next time, stay sharp and keep Rust-ing on!

[END OF SCRIPT]

Please remember to email the script as a PDF document titled "Axum_vs_Rocket_Showdown_in_Rust_Web_Development.pdf" to <PRESIDIO_ANONYMIZED_EMAIL_ADDRESS> with the subject line “Technical Writer Test Task Submission.” 

Good luck with your submission!

**Title: "Axum 0.7.0 | Tokio: Elevating Rust Web Dev to New Heights!"

[INTRO]
Bogdan: Howdy, Rust enthusiasts! Today, we're unleashing a game-changer that's about to turn your Rust web development journey on its head. Buckle up for a wild ride as we dive into the unveiling of Axum 0.7.0, coupled with Tokio—a dynamic duo set to redefine the Rust web development landscape.

[RETENTION HOOK]
Bogdan: But hold on, before we jump into this Rust rollercoaster, here's a question for you: Ever wondered what could turbocharge your Rust web frameworks? Get ready for a revelation that's about to reshape the way you code on the web!

[BODY]
Bogdan: Let's kick things off by taking a stroll down the memory lane of Rust web frameworks. Rocket, the sweetheart of simplicity and elegance, has long reigned supreme in the Rust world. But, my friends, evolution is the heartbeat of progress, and today, we're introducing you to Axum 0.7.0—the rising star in the Rust galaxy.

Bogdan: Powered by the mighty Tokio runtime, Axum is here to revolutionize the way we craft web applications in Rust. But what sets it apart? Well, brace yourselves as we explore how Axum's slick design, combined with Tokio's muscle, unleashes a whole new level of performance, flexibility, and reliability.

[MENTION ONGOING UPDATES]
Bogdan: And here's the kicker—Axum is not just a one-time wonder. With a commitment to continuous updates, it's like having a framework that grows and adapts right alongside your needs. Because, let's face it, in the world of coding, adaptability is key!

[COMPARISON WITH ROCKET]
Bogdan: Now, let's talk turkey about Axum and its friendly rival, Rocket. While Rocket has seen a facelift, Axum's innovation is setting the bar high. The sleek Axum design coupled with Tokio's runtime prowess? That's the new gold standard Rocket's got to keep up with.

Bogdan: Can Rocket match Axum's stride in updates and innovation? That's the million-dollar question. Watch closely as these frameworks evolve, and let's see if Rocket can dance to the rhythm of Axum's revolutionary beat.

[OUTRO]
Bogdan: And now, the grand finale! With Axum 0.7.0 | Tokio, we're ushering in a new era of web development in Rust. Say farewell to the mundane and welcome a world where performance meets simplicity.

Bogdan: As we wrap up this exhilarating news, let me share my take on this monumental leap forward. Axum 0.7.0 | Tokio marks a high point in the Rust ecosystem—a testament to our community's relentless pursuit of progress and innovation.

Bogdan: Before you hit the hay, here's a nudge. Dive into Axum 0.7.0 | Tokio, be at the forefront of this Rust revolution, and don't forget to share your experiences below. Drop a comment, show some love with a like, and hit that subscribe button for more Rusty adventures.

[CTA]
Bogdan: That's a wrap for today's Rust news. Subscribe to our channel for a front-row seat to more thrilling updates and innovations in the world of Rust. Until next time, happy coding, and stay Rusty!

[END]

[INTRO]

[BODY]
Bogdan: "Let's hit rewind for a sec. Rocket has been our go-to for clean, intuitive Rust web dev. Yet, the tech world thrives on innovation, and Axum 0.7.0 is stepping up, embracing the async revolution with open arms and supercharged by the robust Tokio."

Bogdan: "What makes this tag team so groundbreaking? Imagine a framework that not only streamlines your workflow but also pushes the boundaries of what Rust can achieve on the web. That’s Axum for you, folks – designed for the future, delivering today."

[MENTION ONGOING UPDATES]
Bogdan: "Did you think Axum's just resting on its laurels? Think again. Continuous improvement is the name of the game, ensuring that Axum evolves with the developers’ sprawling ambitions and the complex needs of modern web applications."

[COMPARISON WITH ROCKET]
Bogdan: "Alright, let's switch gears and talk shop. Rocket's been getting tune-ups, sure, but Axum 0.7.0 is in the fast lane with a formula one blend of Tokio's async horsepower. It's a wake-up call for Rocket—adapt or be left behind."

Bogdan: "Is it possible for Rocket to soar to the same heights of innovation and finesse that Axum 0.7.0 offers? The race is on, and the Rust community is watching with bated breath."

[OUTRO]
Bogdan: "As we zoom out, it's clear Axum 0.7.0 and Tokio together are more than just an upgrade—they're a paradigm shift in Rust web development, a herald of what's to come."

Bogdan: "This isn't just good news; it's a milestone for Rust’s web dev scene. It's a bright future we’re facing, fellow coders, made brighter by intelligent frameworks like Axum."

Bogdan: "Got thoughts on Axum's rise? Surprises, skepticism, excitement? Light up the comments section! If you're digging the content, smash that Like button and ring the bell for the latest in Rust’s ever-evolving world. And hey, subscribe for a front seat to the Rust revolution."

[CTA]
Bogdan: "Thanks for tuning in to this Rust-fueled news flash. Stay curious, keep experimenting, and as always, stay Rusty! Until next time, happy coding, and may your compile times be ever in your favor."

[END]

this is a transcript of one of his videos and please make easy english

Translating the technical content into easier English can help more viewers understand the material. However, when simplifying technical content, it's crucial to ensure that key information isn't lost or oversimplified to the point of inaccuracy. Here's a version of the transcript in simpler English:

---

00:00:00.280 Okay, if you're learning Rust, you've probably heard of Rocket. It's a big deal in Rust for making websites. But there's been a big problem with it – it's not getting updates fast enough. People have been talking about this on Reddit and other places.

00:00:13.200 Some have even started to wonder if Rocket is still a good choice for Rust users. The good news is, Rocket just got a huge update, and they're also changing how they make decisions about the project. I'll tell you all about that, and then I'll share my own thoughts on whether Rust users should keep using Rocket.

00:00:44.440 Rocket started just a year after Rust was first released and it quickly became popular because it was fast, safe, and easy to use, thanks to Rust's special code called macros. But then, Rocket hit a rough patch and didn't get any big updates for over four years.

00:01:00.160 Sergio, the one guy who made Rocket, said on a GitHub post that personal issues kept him from working on it. But he didn't really tell the Rust community what was going on. So, people started looking at other options, like actix-web and Axum.

00:01:25.520 In May of 2023, Rocket even got taken off a website that recommends the best Rust tools for making websites. Things looked bad for Rocket's future. But then, last week, two important things happened. They announced a new plan for how decisions will be made about Rocket, and they released a new version, 0.5.

00:01:46.759 Sergio admitted that Rocket has had a rocky time recently. But now, to stop this from happening again, he's setting up a non-profit organization to help Rocket grow and to spread out the decision-making. This should keep one person from holding up progress.

00:02:01.960 And at the same time as this announcement, we got Rocket version 0.5 with some cool updates. Rocket now works with the stable version of Rust, which means it's easier for more people to use and it should work better. It also supports async I/O now, which lets you write code that can do lots of things at once. That's a huge improvement for performance and for working with other Rust tools.

00:02:58.440 They also added support for things like streams and websockets. I'm really excited about Rocket's future because it was the first framework that made building servers in Rust fun for me.

00:03:28.519 But now, would I recommend Rocket with its shaky history? If you're already using Rocket, then stick with it because changing frameworks is tough, especially if you have a lot of code. The new update and the new decision-making plan give me hope.

00:03:57.040 But if you're starting fresh, I’d maybe suggest looking at Axum or actix-web which are also good choices. Axum is made by the Tokio team and works well with it. It's popular and growing fast. actix-web is also good; it's been around and is reliable.

00:04:17.279 With the recent good news, I think Rocket is still a solid choice, even for new projects. I was just working on a server project in my Rust boot camp using Rocket, and plans on showing how to do it with Axum too. Now I can have both.

00:04:32.560 If you want to learn how to make a web server with Rocket or Axum, check out the Rust Developer Boot Camp at Let's Ryust.com. And tell me what you think about all this in the comments! Subscribe for more Rust videos. Hope you enjoyed this, and always remember to keep Rusty!

---

Remember to keep key technical concepts while adjusting the language to be more accessible. This script offers a simpler presentation without losing the essence of the content.

Title: "Rocket 0.5: Boosting Rust Web Development!"

[INTRO]
Hey everyone! If you're into Rust, you've likely heard about Rocket. It's a fan favorite for building websites with Rust. But, there's been some talk because updates haven't been coming out as quick as we hoped. Today, though, I've got some great news about Rocket that might just change your mind about it.

[BODY]
So, Rocket's been around for quite some time, and it was awesome because it was fast, secure, and pretty straightforward to use. This was all thanks to Rust's clever coding tricks. But then, Rocket hit a slump—no big changes for years, and the person who made it, Sergio, had a tough time and couldn't work on it much. Not many people knew what was going on.

Because of this silence, some started checking out other options like Axum, which is built by the folks who made Tokio, another Rust tool. But then, out of the blue, Rocket surprised us! They're shaking things up with a new update, Rocket 0.5, and some changes to how they make decisions from now on.

Now, let's talk about the cool stuff Rocket 0.5 brings to the table. First up, it works with the stable version of Rust, which is a big win because it means more people can use it hassle-free, and it's going to be more reliable. Plus, it's now friends with async I/O, meaning it can multitask like a pro which boots performance sky-high.

They've also thrown in new features for streaming and real-time connections with websockets. This has got me pretty hyped for what's ahead for Rocket.

[OUTRO]
Now, if you're already using Rocket, my advice? Hang tight, these new changes make it worth your while. If you're pondering starting fresh, hey, Axum and actix-web are worth a look, but Rocket's not out of the race. I mean, it's the framework that got me hooked on building Rust servers in the first place.

If you're curious about making web servers with Rocket or Axum, swing by my Rust Developer Boot Camp. Drop your thoughts in the comments, and if you want to stay in the loop with all things Rust, hit that subscribe button. Thanks for tuning in, and keep your code Rusty!
refactor this in a manner you mention in the beginning that is the end of rocket most rust framework and the start of axum

Title: "Rust Revolution: Is Rocket's Reign Over as Axum Takes Flight?"

[INTRO]
Hey folks! Are we witnessing the end of an era in Rust web development as Rocket, the once go-to Rust framework, gives way to a new champion? It's time to buckle up and explore the rise of Axum, poised to soar across the Rust skies.

[BODY]

Title: "New Changes in Rust: Rocket Passes the Torch to Axum"

[INTRO]
Hey everyone! In the world of Rust, where we make websites, we might be seeing a big change. Rocket, which used to be everyone's favorite, might not be the top choice anymore. Let's see if Axum will take its place.

[BODY]
For a long time, Rocket was the best bet for making websites with Rust. It was known for being fast, keeping things safe, and being pretty easy to use. But then, things got tough for the person who made Rocket, Sergio, and he couldn't update it as much as before. The Rust community started to miss hearing news about Rocket.

That's when Axum started to shine. Made by the same people who made another Rust tool called Tokio, Axum became popular because it looked new and worked really well with Tokio.

Rocket's not out, though – it just got an important update, Rocket 0.5. It now works with Rust's main version, which means it's easier to use and more reliable for everyone. Rocket can now handle doing more things at once, which is a big improvement for making websites work smoothly and do more cool things like live updates and making connections with websockets.

But the big question is – does this change mean Rocket is stepping back and letting Axum take the lead?

[OUTRO]
If you already work with Rocket, this update is good news and you might want to stick with it. But if you're thinking of starting something new with Rust, you might want to think about Axum.

this is the video content but ithink im missing sometings 
● Intro
○ Hook
■ The goal is to grab the viewer's attention in the first couple of seconds.
■ Using short words or phrases is preferable.
○ Retention hook
■ The job of the retention hook is to make the viewer want to watch the
entire video.
■ A retention hook can be a question that will be answered at the end of the
video, or a statement about something that will be revealed at the end.

● Body
○ History / Context / Background
■ This is the “meat” of the video. The part viewers watch in anticipation of
the retention pay-off.
■ For news videos you can include history, context, or background that
helps the viewer better understand the news story.

○ Transition
■ This should be a short transition that prepares viewers for the retention
pay-off.

● Outro
○ Retention pay-off
■ This is where you give the viewers what you promised in the retention
hook.
■ For news videos this can be the news story itself.
○ Commentary (hope, concern, etc.)
■ Give viewers commentary on the news. Is this news good for Rust?
Should viewers be concerned? Remember that this will be Bogdan
talking.
○ CTA
■ This is where Bogdan gives viewers a call to action (ex: download the
cheat sheet, leave a comment, like the video, etc) and closes out the
video. Refer to the Example Script folder for reference.

[BODY]
History / Context / Background:
"Rocket has been Rust's staple for crafting web applications, known for its speed and user-friendly design. Yet, as the updates became less frequent, the community started to question its sustainability. This is where Axum enters the scene. Born out of the cutting-edge async ecosystem by the Tokio team, Axum promises to harness the full potential of Rust's asynchronous programming."

Transition:
"As we take stock of Rocket's comeback with its awaited upgrade to 0.5, we're faced with a pivotal moment in the Rust web development saga."

Comparison:
"While Rocket has matured, with enhancements like compatibility with stable Rust and the inclusion of async I/O, Axum has been built from the ground up with async in mind. This means Axum offers out-of-the-box support for modern asynchronous web apps, which could be seen as a step beyond Rocket's capabilities. With a focus on type safety, a modular design, and a more declarative interface, Axum is crafted to deliver high concurrency and fast performance, which are critical in today's web-oriented tasks."

[OUTRO]
